title: The Schubert Variety of a Pair of Linear Spaces
speaker: Andrew Berget
abstract:
I will motivate the study of the Schubert variety of a pair of linear spaces via Kempf collapsing of vector bundles. I'll describe equations defining this variety and how this yields a simplicial complex determined by a pair of matroids which generalizes the Schubert variety of the pair of linear spaces.
Toward the end of the talk, I'll discuss how one extends the most interesting properties of the variety to arbitrary pairs of matroids, yielding some beautiful positivity results.

proof_stages.label: Collapsing framework and C/K formulas
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the collapse Y_E of a subbundle E of a trivial bundle, emphasizes the birational case, and records degree and K-polynomial formulas in terms of Chern classes and Euler characteristics.

proof_stages.label: Schubert variety of a pair of linear spaces
proof_stages.summary: For two linear subspaces L1 and L2, the talk defines Y(L1,L2) as the collapse of the associated bundles on the permutohedral toric variety and identifies it with the closure of pairs (tu,tw), setting the goal of computing its degree and K-polynomial.

proof_stages.label: Diagonal Dilworth truncation as matroidal control
proof_stages.summary: The matroid D(M1,M2), built from the Minkowski-sum expression P(M1)+P(M2)-simplex, is introduced as the combinatorial object governing birationality and the later degeneration.

proof_stages.label: Gröbner degeneration, normality, and Stanley-Reisner complex
proof_stages.summary: A theorem gives circuit-parametrized equations and a squarefree initial ideal for Y(L1,L2), proves normality and rational singularities, states the birational rank condition, and packages the initial ideal as a matroid-dependent Stanley-Reisner complex Δ_w(M1,M2).

proof_stages.label: K-polynomial identity for realizable matroid pairs
proof_stages.summary: Applying the K-polynomial proposition to the squarefree degeneration yields an identity between the K-polynomial of Δ_w(M1,M2) and a generating series of Euler characteristics of exterior powers of the associated quotient bundles, first under realizability and the rank hypothesis.

proof_stages.label: Bivaluative extension strategy
proof_stages.summary: Since the Euler-characteristic side is bivaluative in the two matroids, the speaker reduces extension to all matroids to proving that the Stanley-Reisner K-polynomial is also bivaluative, using agreement on characteristic-zero realizable pairs.

proof_stages.label: Fan-displacement extraction of the K-polynomial
proof_stages.summary: Products of matroid Chern classes are computed by the fan displacement rule, producing a cell-and-monomial construction from which enough face data of Δ_w(M1,M2) can be recovered to express its K-polynomial manifestly bivaluatively.

examples.name: Grassmannian collapse to a determinantal variety
examples.note: The tautological subbundle on a Grassmannian, repeated as a direct sum, collapses to the variety of k-by-n matrices of rank at most r, making the general collapse construction concrete without being used in the later proof.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker runs the collapsing construction on the tautological bundle over a Grassmannian, takes k direct sums, identifies the image as k-by-n matrices of rank at most r, and discusses minors/birationality.

examples.name: Reciprocal plane from the wonderful compactification
examples.note: The line bundle pulled back from the wonderful compactification of a linear space collapses to its reciprocal plane, and the speaker uses it to illustrate how the degree and K-polynomial propositions recover known matroidal formulas.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker constructs the collapse from the wonderful compactification as the closure of coordinatewise inverses and later computes its degree and K-polynomial via the propositions.

examples.name: Usual Schubert variety as the one-dimensional second space case
examples.note: Taking L2 to be the span of the all-ones vector specializes the pair construction to the affine cone over the usual Schubert variety, explaining the terminology and comparing the new object to a familiar one.
examples.engagement_reason: The one-dimensional second-space case is stated as giving the affine cone over the usual Schubert variety, but no calculation or construction is carried out for that specialization.

passes.reason: Taking the second space to be one-dimensional is the minimal boundary case that collapses the pair construction to the usual Schubert variety.

examples.name: Speyer-invariant specialization M1 = M2
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains that the diagonal case relates to Speyer's matroid invariant, while separating M1 and M2 makes the hidden valuativity visible.
examples.engagement_reason: The M1 = M2 Speyer specialization is discussed as the eventual motivating case and contrasted with the two-matroid setup, but the speaker does not compute or verify anything in that specialization.

examples.name: Three-matroid and U_{1,n} variants
examples.note: A question about adding a third matroid is used to illustrate that the two-matroid construction is special because the resulting polytope remains matroidal, whereas three summands usually lead to polymatroids except in simpler cases such as adding U_{1,n}.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the polytope construction to three matroids and then to the M1, M2, U_{1,n} case, explaining the resulting Minkowski-sum-minus-simplex expression and the simplex cancellation.

passes.reason: The general three-matroid extension is cited as an obstruction to staying matroidal, while the U_{1,n} subcase is the rank-one uniform minimal symmetric workaround.
The general three-matroid attempt is cited as failing to remain a matroid, while the U_{1,n} workaround is the rank-one uniform simplex with maximal S_n symmetry.
